pattern: The base subsequence count is a running-count sweep (each pattern[1] closes a subsequence with every pattern[0] seen so far), and the single extra character only ever helps at one end, so comparing an inserted-front bonus against an inserted-back bonus finds the best single addition.
hints: Without adding anything, sweep left to right counting how many times pattern[0] has appeared, and every time you see pattern[1], add that running count to the answer (if the two characters are the same, this single sweep already counts everything correctly).

The one character you get to add is worth the most either at the very front (as an extra pattern[0], gaining one subsequence per existing pattern[1] in the whole string) or at the very back (as an extra pattern[1], gaining one subsequence per existing pattern[0] in the whole string) - it is never worth inserting anywhere in the middle.
Compute the base count with the sweep, then separately count total occurrences of pattern[0] and of pattern[1] in the string; the answer is base + max(count of pattern[1], count of pattern[0]).

edge_inputs.description: distinct pattern characters, inserting the front character wins
text has no occurrence of either pattern character at all
pattern[1] never appears, so every added pattern[0] before it is wasted unless the addition is pattern[1] at the very end
text already ends with many pattern[1] copies, so appending another pattern[0] would not help versus appending pattern[1]

stress.description: 300-character random string over a 4-letter alphabet including both pattern letters, exercising the running-count sweep
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    alphabet = 'abcd'
    text = ''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(n))
    p0, p1 = rng.sample(alphabet, 2) if rng.random() < 0.8 else (rng.choice(alphabet), rng.choice(alphabet))
    return [text, p0 + p1]
stress.note: mostly distinct pattern letters with an occasional identical-letter pattern to exercise both formula branches
